revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:奉幣]]
{{複数の問題|独自研究=April 22, 2015 (Wed) 12:25 (UTC)|Referencesの明記=April 22, 2015 (Wed) 12:25 (UTC)|正確性=April 22, 2015 (Wed) 12:25 (UTC)}} '''Hōhei''' (奉幣, ほうべい、ほうへい) refers to the act of offering ritual paper money and cloth ({{ill|heihaku|ja|幣帛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/heihaku|1=Hanpei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11481885}}) to a deity. In Japan, it is of great importance to present heihaku to shrines ({{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=jinja|1=Shinto shrine}}), and for the 3,132 deities listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Shinmeichō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Shinmeichō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, either official offerings ({{ill|kanpei|ja|官幣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 幣帛|1=Hanpei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11481885}}) from the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|qid=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities|1=Department of Divinities}} or national offerings ({{ill|kokuhē|ja|国幣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kokuhē|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) from the {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|qid=Q1351234|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi (official)}} were presented.
Furthermore, at particularly important shrines, imperial envoys known as {{ill|chokushi|ja|勅使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Chokushi|qid=Q11399920}} were dispatched to conduct the offering ceremony, and these envoys were known as '''Hōhei-shi'''. Although their number varied over time, by the mid-11th century the system had come to include the {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|qid=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}}. It was stipulated that Hōhei-shi must be of the fifth Rank or higher and be deemed to have attained the divine will through divination. In some shrines the selection of the Hōhei-shi was fixed—for example, at {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Jingū|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, members of the royal ({{ill|Shirakawa-ke|ja|白川家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa-ke|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 白川伯王家|1=Shirakawa family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11579697}}) family were dispatched, at {{ill|Usa Jingū|en|Usa Jingū|qid=Q715632|lt=Ujigami Jingū|1=Usa Jingū}} members of the {{ill|Waki-shi|ja|和気氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waki-shi|1=Wake clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11417729}}, and at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|qid=Q714559|lt=Kasuga Taisha|1=Kasuga-taisha}} members of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|en|Fujiwara clan|qid=Q641628|lt=Fujiwara-shi|1=Fujiwara clan}} were sent. Typically, a messenger of the imperial decree ({{ill|senmei|ja|宣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/senmei|1=Senmyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6139345}}) accompanied the Hōhei-shi and, after the offering, reported the emperor’s decree.
From the medieval period onward, the offerings to Ise Jingū during its {{ill|Kannamesai Festival|en|Kannamesai Festival|qid=Q11255045|lt=Kannamesai|1=Kannamesai Festival}} came to be specifically known as '''Reihei''' (例幣). The envoys dispatched for these offerings were called '''Reihei-shi''' (alternatively, to distinguish them from the later Nikko Reihei-shi, they were sometimes also referred to as {{ill|Ise Reihei-shi|ja|伊勢例幣使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Reihei-shi|qq=}}). Moreover, special offerings sent on the occasions of the emperor’s accession, the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|qid=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai|1=Daijosai}}, or coming-of-age ceremonies, which were reported to Ise Jingū and other shrines, were called '''Yoshino Hōhei''' (由奉幣).
== Edo Period == With the decline of the imperial court, the offerings from the court gradually diminished and became largely formalized. After the onset of the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|qid=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}}, with the exception of those to Ise Jingū, offerings were no longer made. From the mid-17th century, as the {{ill|Edo Bakufu|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo Bakufu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} began to place greater emphasis on court rituals, offerings were revived in the first year of the {{ill|Enkyō (Edo period)|en|Enkyō (Edo period)|qid=Q1183728|lt=Enkyō era|1=Enkyō (Edo period)}} (1744) to the upper seven shrines among the {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|qid=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} after an absence of approximately 300 years.
=== Nikko Reihei-shi === (See also: {{ill|Nikko Reihei-shi Kaidō|ja|日光例幣使街道#日光例幣使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikko Reihei-shi Kaidō|1=Nikkō Reiheishi Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3069778}}) Beginning in the third year of the {{ill|Shōhō|en|Shōhō|qid=Q1146192|lt=Shōhō era|1=Shōhō}} (1646), a system was established whereby envoys known as '''Nikko Reihei-shi''' were dispatched to the annual festival at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qid=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}. In the Edo period, the term "Reihei-shi" was most commonly used to refer specifically to the Nikko envoys.
For the Nikko Reihei-shi, the journey to Nikko at that time was an arduous "rural expedition," and there was a strong desire to complete the offering and return as quickly as possible. Moreover, if the route passed through {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|qid=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}}, the additional formalities of greeting the bakufu would be burdensome. Consequently, on the outward journey, the Reihei-shi would travel via an inland route—using the {{ill|Nakasendō|en|Nakasendō|qid=Q1154479|lt=Nakasendō|1=Nakasendō}}, passing through {{ill|Kuragano-shuku|en|Kuragano-shuku|qid=Q3200519|lt=Kuragano-shuku|1=Kuragano-shuku}}, and then along the designated Reihei-shi Kaidō—to reach Nikko without transiting through Edo or the {{ill|Tōkaidō (region)|en|Tōkaidō (region)|qid=Q1196306|lt=Tōkaidō|1=Tōkaidō (region)}}. On the return trip, the customary route was to travel southward on the {{ill|Nikkō Kaidō|en|Nikkō Kaidō|qid=Q511576|lt=Nikkō Kaidō|1=Nikkō Kaidō}}, pass through Edo, and rejoin the Tōkaidō on the way back to Kyoto; however, in both the 5th year of the {{ill|Anyō era|ja|安永|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anyō era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 安永 (元号)|1=An'ei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1193910}} (1776) and the 14th year of the {{ill|Tenpō|en|Tenpō|qid=Q1369045|lt=Tempō era|1=Tenpō}} (1843), the return journey also utilized the Nakasendō. In the early Meiji period, the former Nikko Kaidō (Mibu-dō) was referred to as the "Old Reihei-shi Kaidō" in rapid survey maps.
Because the Reihei-shi, as described above, carried the authority that bridged the imperial court and the bakufu, their processions eventually grew very large, imposing a heavy burden on the bakufu and the post stations along the route. Owing to the religious authority vested in them, the envoys would distribute small offerings of rice (bags containing a small amount of washed rice) to people along the route and collect money in return; similarly, inns would give out strips of paper, colored paper, or fans, again receiving money in exchange. Many courtiers who became Reihei-shi were financially destitute, so serving in this role provided them with a source of temporary income. Eventually, some of the entourage of the Reihei-shi began to behave arrogantly, riding in palanquins with an air of authority. To counter this, prearranged payments known as "Nyūkon-kin" were given by the post towns to deter such behavior. In an extreme example, it is said that some envoys would deliberately shake their palanquins and then accuse the bearers of having disrespected the imperial envoy, thereby extorting money; this is purported to be the origin of the term "yusuru" (to shake).<ref>{{Sfn|深井|1997|pp=144-147}}</ref>
== Meiji Period == In the 41st year of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} (1908), the ''Kōshitsu Saishirei'' (Imperial Ritual Order) established detailed regulations regarding hōhei. In the 44th year of the Meiji era (1911), the formal title of the envoys was set as '''Heihaku Kōshin-shi'''.
== Postwar == After World War II, offerings (hōhei) are still made for the annual festivals of shrines under the imperial ritual system ({{ill|Chokusaisha|en|Chokusaisha|qid=Q175288|lt=Chokusaisha|1=Chokusaisha}}) such as Ise Jingū, as well as for the regular anniversary festivals of mountain tombs. In these cases, officials connected with the {{ill|Shōten-shoku|ja|掌典職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōten-shoku|1=Chief Cleric of the Imperial Household|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11498167}} assume the role of Hōhei-shi.
Additionally, the envoys responsible for delivering heihaku from the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|Association of Shinto Shrines|qid=Q908077|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} to subordinate shrines are known as {{ill|Kenpei-shi|ja|献幣使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenpei-shi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 幣帛|1=Hanpei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11481885}}.
== References・脚注 ==
{{Reflist|18em}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=深井甚三|authorlink=深井甚三|title=江戸の旅人たち|date=1997-02-01|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4346755}}|location={{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}{{ill|Bunkyō|en|Bunkyō|qid=Q212713|lt=Bunkyō-ku|1=Bunkyō}}|series=歴史文化ライブラリー; 9|language=日本語|isbn=4-642-05409-X|ref={{SfnRef|深井|1997}} }}
= == See Also === * [[Bakumatsu Hyakuwa]] – Contains recollections of the Nikko Hōhei-shi.
{{citation needed|ほうへい}} {{translated page|ja|奉幣|version=98605873|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:奉幣]]
<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11445508}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
